summary: Nissl bodies represent rough endoplasmic reticulum and are found in the neuronal cell body and dendrites, but are absent from the axon hillock and axon.
Nissl bodies are found throughout the neuron including the axon.
Nissl bodies represent smooth endoplasmic reticulum.
In the PNS, Schwann cells produce myelin, with each Schwann cell myelinating a single internode of one axon.
In the PNS, oligodendrocytes produce myelin around peripheral nerves.
A single Schwann cell myelinates multiple axons simultaneously.
In the CNS, oligodendrocytes produce myelin and a single oligodendrocyte can myelinate internodes of multiple axons.
In the CNS, Schwann cells produce myelin around central axons.
Each oligodendrocyte myelinates only one axon.
The three connective tissue sheaths of a peripheral nerve are epineurium (outermost), perineurium (around fascicles), and endoneurium (around individual fibres).
The outermost sheath of a peripheral nerve is the endoneurium.
Perineurium surrounds individual nerve fibres, not fascicles.
The blood-nerve barrier is formed primarily by the perineurium, which has tight junctions between its cells.
The blood-nerve barrier is formed by the epineurium.
There is no blood-nerve barrier in peripheral nerves.
Guillain-Barre syndrome is caused by autoimmune demyelination of peripheral nerves, leading to ascending paralysis and prolonged distal motor latencies.
Guillain-Barre syndrome is caused by axonal degeneration in the CNS.
Guillain-Barre syndrome affects only sensory nerves.
Astrocytes are the most abundant glial cells in the CNS and contribute to the blood-brain barrier through their perivascular foot processes.
Microglia are the most abundant glial cells in the CNS.
Astrocytes produce myelin in the CNS.
